moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Clemenz Opening, an unusual first move that immediately signals we're not following standard opening theory. By advancing the h-pawn, we create luft for our king and prepare g4 to control the center indirectly from the flanks. This move is rare but playable—it's an interesting sideline that can surprise opponents who aren't prepared for flank-based strategies.

Black mirrors our flank strategy and challenges our g4 plans immediately. By pushing the h-pawn, Black stakes a claim on the kingside and prevents us from easily advancing our g-pawn without creating weaknesses. This symmetrical response is a sensible way to meet our unusual opening.

Now we advance with g4, committing to aggressive flank play in the Spike Lee Gambit. We're offering to trade pawns (if Black captures with hxg4) while gaining space and opening lines on the kingside—notice how g4 controls the important h3 and f3 squares. The idea is that after we've weakened Black's kingside with pawn moves, we can launch a kingside attack or exploit the loose dark squares around Black's king.
